name: pc-screen-control
description: A screenshot is a picture made for human eyes. This hands you the screen as data instead - so you press the right button by name, know it worked, and hardly ever need the user's mouse to do it.
long_description: Use this INSTEAD of taking a screenshot and clicking a coordinate. Windows already publishes what is on screen as structured data - every button, field and list with its name and state, the same thing screen readers read - and this hands it to you directly. That means three things. You press the right thing, because controls are found by name and not by pixel, so nothing drifts when a window moves. You know whether it worked, because every action returns the element's state before and after. And the user keeps their mouse, because reading the tree and operating controls go through the accessibility interface rather than the pointer - they can carry on working while you work. Start every desktop task with describe_screen. 35 tools: read the tree, operate controls, read tables as rows and columns, set sliders to an exact number, move and resize windows, read and write the clipboard, open menus. Coordinate input and screen capture remain as a documented fallback for surfaces that genuinely paint themselves, such as editing canvases and games. Prefer the pattern route always: no tool steps down to the keyboard or the pointer without saying so, and invoke refuses rather than clicking behind your back, so reach for click, drag or send_keys only when a tool has told you there is no cheaper way. When you do take the mouse or keyboard while the user is active, an input guard warns them with a pulse at the screen edge, holds their input for the moment you need it, and restores their window, focus and text cursor afterwards; set_guard priority:'me' makes you wait for their go instead. check_for_update is the only tool that reaches the network - call it with only_if_due:true at most once per session, and never nag. Windows only.
